narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates an 85% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have a solid LSAT score of 37, just above the typical student's 36.7, along with a high family income bracket, which are positive aspects of your profile. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 falls below the average of 3.21, and your school decile rankings—2nd for Year 1 grades and 1st for Year 3—are notably lower than the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, signaling weaker academic performance in those periods. As a White female planning full-time studies, your demographics align closely with the majority in our dataset, where Whites make up 84%, females 44%, and full-timers 93%. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your LSAT score, which nudged the prediction toward failure despite being slightly above average—likely because it wasn't standout enough to offset other concerns. Next in influence came your Year 1 decile, where your low ranking pushed heavily toward expecting a bar failure. Your even lower Year 3 decile ranked third in impact, reinforcing that trajectory. Your high family income followed, surprisingly contributing toward the failure prediction in your case, perhaps due to how it interacts with your academics. Your undergraduate GPA and racial background had moderate influence, both tilting against passing, while gender and full-time status had the smallest roles, still leaning negative. Overall, these factors combined to outweigh any positives, making your profile riskier than most admitted students who typically show stronger, consistent grades and higher test scores. To boost your future chances, focus on actionable steps like targeted LSAT prep for a retake if possible, building a stronger academic record through better study habits or additional coursework, and seeking mentorship to address past performance dips—these could realistically shift your predicted odds toward passing the bar. You're capable of turning this around with dedication, and many students improve dramatically by addressing these core areas. Keep pursuing your goals; reapplying with enhancements could make all the difference.
